steps.prompt: Which word is a relative pronoun?

steps.reveal: quem = whom (acc. masc. sg., object of 'invenerit')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'qui' — 'quem' with -em is accusative

hints.to sell: That would be 'vendere' — 'venerit' comes from 'venire' (to come)
hints.to hunt: That would be 'venari' — 'venerit' comes from 'venire' (to come)
hints.to conquer: That would be 'vincere' — 'venerit' comes from 'venire' (to come)

steps.reveal: venerit = he shall have come (perf. subj., in temporal clause with 'cum')

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'venit' — 'venerit' with -eri- after 'cum' is perfect subjunctive
hints.future perfect indicative: Future perfect is identical in form but 'cum' requires subjunctive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'veniat' — 'venerit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vendo, vendere, vendidi, venditum: These are the parts of 'vendere' (to sell)
hints.venor, venari, venatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'venari' (to hunt)

steps.reveal: invenerit = he shall have found (perf. subj.)

hints.perfect indicative: In the relative/temporal clause context, 'invenerit' is subjunctive
hints.future perfect indicative: Future perfect looks identical but the subordinate clause calls for subjunctive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'inveniat' — 'invenerit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the parts of 'venire' (to come) — 'invenire' is a compound in + venire
hints.reperio, reperire, repperi, repertum: These are the parts of 'reperire' (to find) — a synonym
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)

steps.reveal: facientem = doing (pres. act. part., acc. masc. sg., agreeing with 'quem')

hints.perfect passive participle: Perfect passive would be 'factum' — 'facientem' with -nt- is present active
hints.future active participle: Future active would be 'facturum' — 'facientem' is present active
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'faciendum' — 'facientem' is present active participle

hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'serve' — 'servus' is nominative subject
Vocative would be 'domine' — 'dominus' is nominative, subject

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used after 'cum'?

hints.'cum' always requires subjunctive: 'Cum' can take indicative (cum temporale) or subjunctive — here it is circumstantial
hints.it expresses a wish: A wish would use 'utinam' + subjunctive — 'cum' + subj. is temporal/circumstantial
hints.it expresses an indirect question: An indirect question uses interrogative words — 'cum' is temporal here

translation: Blessed is that servant whom the master, when he comes, finds so doing
